quiz.title: Entry Quiz: Basic OS and Security Concepts
quiz.description: Assess prerequisite knowledge before Session 05 on Endpoint Security Technologies

question.text: What is the primary difference between a process and a program in an operating system?

options.option: A program is stored on disk; a process is the running instance of that program in memory
A process is stored on disk; a program runs in memory
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable
A program can only run once; a process can run multiple times

question.explanation: A program is a static set of instructions stored in a file (e.g., an .exe or ELF binary). A process is the running, active instance of a program — it has memory allocated, a PID, CPU time, open file handles, and a security context. Multiple processes can be spawned from the same program.

question.text: On a Linux system, where is the system authentication log (recording SSH logins, sudo usage, and PAM events) located on a Debian/Ubuntu system?

options.option: /var/log/syslog
/var/log/auth.log
/var/log/messages
/etc/pam.d/auth

question.explanation: /var/log/auth.log is the authentication log on Debian/Ubuntu systems. On RHEL/CentOS systems it is /var/log/secure. /var/log/syslog and /var/log/messages contain general system messages. /etc/pam.d/ is a configuration directory, not a log file.

question.text: Which Windows component is responsible for managing and enforcing user authentication, group membership, and access tokens for all processes?

options.option: Windows Firewall
Task Scheduler
Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS)
Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI)

question.explanation: LSASS (lsass.exe) is the Windows process responsible for enforcing security policies, handling logon authentication, and managing access tokens. It stores credential material (password hashes, Kerberos tickets) in memory — which is why it is the primary target of credential dumping tools like Mimikatz.

question.text: In a Unix/Linux system, what does a file permission of rwxr-xr-x mean?

options.option: Owner: read/write/execute; Group: read/execute; Others: read/execute
Owner: read/write; Group: read/execute; Others: execute only
Owner: read/write/execute; Group: read/write/execute; Others: read only
All users have read/write/execute permissions

question.explanation: Unix permissions are represented in three groups of three characters (rwx) for Owner, Group, and Others. rwx=read+write+execute, r-x=read+execute (no write), r-x=read+execute. So rwxr-xr-x means: Owner can read/write/execute; Group can read/execute; Others can read/execute.

question.text: What is a scheduled task (Windows) or cron job (Linux)?

options.option: A process that runs continuously in the background as a service
A command or program configured to run automatically at a specified time or trigger
A system call that allocates memory for a process
A network configuration that routes traffic to specific ports

question.explanation: Scheduled tasks (Windows) and cron jobs (Linux) are time-based or event-based automation mechanisms. They run specified commands at configured times or intervals without user intervention. In security contexts, they are a common persistence mechanism — attackers add malicious commands to execute automatically on reboot or at regular intervals.
